title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: Science Belongs to Everyone: The Rise of Citizen Research
content.text: On a Tuesday evening in Bristol, a retired school teacher named Margaret is crouched in her garden with a magnifying glass, logging the beetles she finds under a rotting log. Her observations, uploaded to an app before bedtime, will join a database of millions of similar records collected by amateur naturalists across the country. Margaret is a citizen scientist. (37) [37] Los ciudadanos científicos están cambiando la investigación. [/37] Together, volunteers like her are transforming the scale and speed at which ecological data can be gathered. || The concept of citizen science is not new. Amateur naturalists contributed enormously to nineteenth-century biology: Charles Darwin's correspondence network included hundreds of non-professional observers whose field notes informed his theories. (38) [38] Los aficionados han contribuido a la ciencia durante siglos. [/38] What is new is the digital infrastructure that now connects millions of contributors and allows their data to be processed and analysed in real time. || Projects range from galaxy classification to monitoring air quality. The Galaxy Zoo project, launched in 2007, invited members of the public to examine telescope images and sort galaxies by shape. (39) [39] Los voluntarios clasificaron galaxias en masa con notable precisión. [/39] Within a year, over a hundred thousand people had classified more than fifty million galaxy images — a task that would have taken professional astronomers decades to complete alone. || Recruitment and retention of volunteers present ongoing challenges. (40) [40] Mantener el interés de los voluntarios requiere diseño cuidadoso. [/40] Researchers have found that projects designed like games, with clear tasks, instant feedback and visible progress, keep participants engaged far longer than those that feel like data entry. || Data quality is another concern. Critics note that amateur observations may be inconsistent or poorly recorded. (41) [41] Sin embargo, los métodos de validación reducen significativamente el error. [/41] Experienced volunteers verify the entries of newer contributors, and algorithms flag unusual records for expert review. Studies have shown that, with these checks in place, citizen science data is reliable enough for peer-reviewed publication. || As smartphone cameras, GPS and AI-assisted identification tools improve, the potential of citizen science continues to grow. (42) [42] La tecnología amplía las posibilidades de participación ciudadana en la ciencia. [/42] Margaret, for her part, has no plans to stop. 'I may only be logging beetles,' she says, 'but I know they matter.'

paragraphs.A: In fact, some of the most significant discoveries of the Victorian era would not have been possible without them.
paragraphs.B: Projects that feel purposeful and personally rewarding tend to attract and retain the most committed volunteers.
paragraphs.C: The task proved so popular that the website crashed within hours of launch.
paragraphs.D: Cross-checking systems and peer validation have made it possible to filter out errors with a high degree of confidence.
paragraphs.E: Barriers to participation are falling as technology makes data collection accessible to virtually anyone with a mobile phone.
paragraphs.F: Keeping volunteers motivated once the initial excitement fades is one of the greatest practical difficulties project organisers face.
paragraphs.G: Their collective contribution is quietly reshaping what professional scientists believe is possible.

questions.explanation: G summarises the collective impact of volunteers before explaining how they transform ecological data collection.
A reinforces the historical role of amateurs, connecting the mention of Darwin with the idea that citizen science is not new.
C adds the detail of the website's collapse due to immediate success, before the figures on classifications in a year.
F introduces the problem of retaining volunteers in the long term, which project design studies seek to resolve.
D explains the cross-validation systems that respond to criticisms about data quality.
E links technological improvement with greater accessibility to citizen scientific participation.
